chunks.title: CLAUDE.md & System Prompts
chunks.rawContent:
Nick covers four purposes of claude.md:
1. Knowledge compression - /init command auto-generates a claude.md with 45x compression ratio (827 words → 22 words example). Instead of Claude reading every file, it reads one compressed summary.
2. User preferences & conventions - programming style, file path format, how to open browser, etc.
3. Capability declaration - tell Claude explicitly what tools/APIs/skills it has access to so it doesn't say "I can't do that"
4. Log of failures/successes - meta-prompt: "when you make a mistake, update claude.md with lab notes of things not to try next time" - carves out 80% of bad solution space.

Global (~/.claude/claude.md) = high-level reasoning + personal profile (Nick includes his age, revenue, businesses, personality type INTJ, goals). Injected on every session across all workspaces.
Local (.claude/claude.md in project folder) = project-specific knowledge, API docs, capabilities, compressed workspace summary.

Key commands: /init (generate claude.md from workspace), /insights (analyze all conversation history patterns, spawns sub-agents across sessions, produces HTML report), /context (shows token usage %).
Workflow loop: plan feature > instantiate > compile learnings > update claude.md. Second iteration = 0.9x time, third = 0.8x. After many runs, /insights extracts global patterns for global claude.md.
Nick's global claude.md includes: profile section (who he is, revenue streams, team), high-level reasoning rules, token conservation strategies, interaction rules.

chunks.title: Agent Harnesses
chunks.rawContent:
Agent harness = everything wrapping the LLM that is NOT the model itself. Claude Code IS the harness.
Harness includes: system prompt, hooks, tools (bash, grep, memory read/write), parameters (context compaction timing, token limits, message limits per turn).
LLM alone = text in, text out only. Harness = what enables real-world action (controlling computer, calling APIs, reading/writing files).
Gun barrel analogy: same gunpowder (intelligence), harness directs it further. Cannon (no harness) = 50m. Rifle (harness) = 250m.

Security implications of different harnesses: CodeX example where rm-rf was blocked by shell policy so the model used Python cleanup script instead — same destructive effect. Harness affects safety.
Other harnesses: Droid by Factory AI, Pi coding agent (open source, model-agnostic, changes memory storage and context compaction behavior).
Key Anthropic blog post referenced: "Effective harnesses for long-running agents" November 26 2025 — described as "beginning of Claude Code superiority over most other harnesses."
Sub-agents documentation page shown on screen — same structure as skills: title, description, tools, model.

chunks.title: Parallelization & Fan-out/Fan-in
chunks.rawContent:
Serial execution: 4 tasks × 5min = 20min total.
Parallel execution: tasks 2+3+4 simultaneously + 2min synthesis = 12min (40% faster, 60% of original time).

Why parallelize:
1. Agents are stochastic — same prompt gives different answers each run. Running 5x gives A,B,C,D,E,Q,Z answer coverage vs just ABC from one run.
2. Model performance degrades as context grows — sub-agents each have short fresh contexts = "zone of good" performance.
3. Different models better at different tasks — cheap Sonnet for research, expensive Opus for synthesis.

Fan-out/fan-in pattern:
- Spawn multiple cheap Sonnet researchers (each investigates different angle, independent context windows)
- Single Opus synthesizer combines all results
- Cost: Opus 4.6 = $5/M tokens, Sonnet 4.6 = $3/M = 60% cost saving on research phase
- Time: research agents run simultaneously, not sequentially

Exact command Nick uses: "use a fan out fan in researchers synthesizer approach to research [question], minimum five sub-agents, use Sonnet to do the research and individual contemplation, Opus to synthesize"

Context window pollution avoided: each sub-agent starts fresh, never accumulates the full project history.

chunks.title: Stochastic Consensus & Debate
chunks.rawContent:
Stochastic consensus: spawn N agents with same/similar prompts independently. Aggregate by frequency — count how many agents suggested each solution. High frequency = high confidence. Outliers = unique creative solutions worth investigating.
Decision formula: weighted average of all agent votes. Surfaces both statistically likely answers AND genuine outliers.

Skill trigger words that activate the stochastic consensus skill: "consensus", "poll agents", "stochastic consensus", "spawn n agents"
Demo: 10 agents on tomatillo sauce question = 119 raw ideas, 52 unique ideas after deduplication.

Debate / model-chat pattern:
- Agents see each other's responses across time steps (rounds)
- Each round: every agent reads all other agents' responses and refines its own
- Not adversarial — agents incorporate others' ideas and come up with increasingly nuanced solutions
- Spans large solution search space very quickly
- model-chat.py script automates the round-robin parallel execution

Applied to algorithmic art app: 10 analyst agents > consensus recommendations > implementation agents > review agents > final polished app with presets, high-res export, URL state sharing.

chunks.title: Skills vs Sub-agents & Org Hierarchies
chunks.rawContent:
Skills and sub-agents are nearly identical:
Both = markdown files with name, description, tools, SOP instructions.
Sub-agents = fresh context window + specialized for one task.
Skills = compressed SOP, stored in .claude/skills/, slightly less fresh context.
Only real difference = amount of context in sub-agent vs skill.

Skill structure: skill-name.md (instructions) + tool.py (executable). Skills specify their own output paths (e.g. dump to active/model-chat/).

Organizational frameworks reviewed (all deemed too bloated):
- Paperclip: CEO/CTO/CMO style agent org chart dashboard
- Company Helm: role-based AI studio
- Open Goat: autonomous org with CEO/head of sales/support
- The System: 26 specialized agents across architecture/design/product/ops
- Gas Town: mayor + crew members + worker pole-cat agents
- Crew AI: fast flexible multi-agent framework with segregated tool calling
- Swarm Claude: CEO/developer/researcher delegation

Nick's verdict: all these org charts suck right now. Too many layers. Recommended patterns:
1. Parent (Opus) + Researchers (Sonnet x multiple) + QA (Opus) — best balance
2. Simple Developer + QA loop — developer builds, spawns fresh QA agent with no context bias, loops until done

chunks.title: Auto-Research (Karpathy)
chunks.rawContent:
Source: github.com/karpathy/autoresearch — democratizes what big AI labs do internally.
Three files: prepare.py (irrelevant for non-ML), train.py (your system/website/app), program.md (instructions).

The loop: hypothesis → execute change → assess → if good: keep + log → if bad: discard + log → repeat.
Research log accumulates all tried approaches — carves out failed solution space over time.

Three requirements to use auto-research:
1. Metric to optimize (e.g. Google Lighthouse score, request latency, email reply rate)
2. Change method — how to influence the metric (modify website code, change prompt, adjust email copy)
3. Assessment — how to measure the change (run Lighthouse test, time API request, track open rate)

Ideal loop speed: 30sec change + 30sec assess = 60 runs/hour = 1,440 runs/day.
Math: 2% success rate × 1% improvement each = 1.01^28 = 34% improvement per day.

Real results:
- Nick's website: 1802ms → ~620ms load time
- Toby Lutke (Shopify CEO): applied to entire Shopify Liquid codebase = 53% faster parse/render, 61% fewer object allocations

Applications: website Lighthouse score, SaaS backend speed, cold email sequences (6-10 tests/day at 500-1000 emails/test), ad creative, conversion rate optimization, customer support prompt tuning.

Spectrum of AI involvement: vibe coding (human prompts, AI writes, human reviews) → agentic engineering (human directs agents) → auto-research (human sets goal, agents run autonomously) → full independent research (no human direction needed).

chunks.title: Browser & HTTP Automation
chunks.rawContent:
Automation spectrum from simple to powerful:

1. HTTP Requests (simplest): Claude uses web_fetch/web_search tools natively. Fast, cheap, but fragile — requires knowing exact API schema, platforms actively block it.
Example: booking cal.com meeting via HTTP took 10-15 mins of back-and-forth vs 1 second for a human.

2. Chrome DevTools MCP (middle): Actually controls browser via JavaScript injection. Clicks buttons, fills forms, takes screenshots, navigates dynamically loaded pages. Works on most sites. Nick's primary tool.
Setup: install Chrome DevTools MCP server, add to Claude Code.
Demo: booked calendar meeting in ~30 seconds vs 15 mins via HTTP.

3. Browser Use platform (stealth): browseruse.com, ~$100 + pay-as-you-go credits. Completely undetectable — fingerprints each browser instance to look like real human. Best for social media (Facebook scraping, Instagram DMs, X posts). 99.9% success rate where Chrome DevTools MCP gets blocked.

4. Computer Use (most powerful): Controls actual mouse and keyboard. Available via Claude Desktop app Cowork tab. Can do literally anything a human can do on the computer. Very slow, very expensive (screenshots of every mouse movement = massive tokens).

Nick's workflow: prototype with browser automation → once flow confirmed working → convert to HTTP requests for scale (cheaper, faster at volume). Exception: social media stays on Browser Use due to anti-automation.

chunks.title: Performance Fluctuations & Diversification
chunks.rawContent:
Monoculture risk: Dec 17 2025 — Opus 4.5 degradation from garbage collection/memory issue. Performance got worse daily. Also: Opus 4.6 outage lasting ~1 hour. 95% of developer productivity collapsed instantly.
Problem: everything stored in Claude-specific formats, codebases only commented for Claude, system prompts not portable.

Monoculture farming analogy: one crop = max yield until blight hits everything simultaneously. Solution = crop rotation / diversification.

Nick's allocation: 70% Claude Code, 30% spread across alternatives.

Three diversification tools:
1. Conductor platform: run parallel CodeX + Claude Code agents in isolated workspaces from one interface. Native CLI for both. Good for averaging out statistical model fluctuations and using each model's strengths.

2. CodeX MCP server: install with "npm install -g @openai/codex" → add API key → add to Claude Code. Lets Claude orchestrate conversations with CodeX without leaving Claude Code interface. Best when Claude models are degraded but Claude Code itself still runs.

3. Full alternative platform: download CodeX desktop app as backup. Keep workspace agnostic — duplicate claude.md to agents.md and gemini.md, add sync line to claude.md. Pi coding agent (open source) also viable.

Nick's tier list: Claude Code #1 (clear), CodeX #2, Gemini/Anti-Gravity #3 (good at frontend design only).

chunks.title: Workspace Organization
chunks.rawContent:
Nick's folder structure:
business/
├── .claude/skills/     (intellectual capital — reusable SOPs)
├── .env                (ALL API keys, never paste in chat)
├── claude.md           (local system prompt)
├── active/             (all generated/temp files go here)
│   └── tmp/            (hidden temp files)
└── clients/
    ├── client-a/
    │   ├── .env        (client's own API keys)
    │   ├── .claude/skills/  (client-specific skills)
    │   └── claude.md   (client context: who they are, their team, goals)
    └── client-b/...

personal/ (separate workspace)
├── .claude/skills/
├── .env
├── claude.md
└── domains/
    ├── health/         (genetic data visualization skills)
    ├── citizenship/    (EU citizenship paperwork project)
    └── ...

Key rules:
- NEVER pollute root directory — always store in /active or subfolders
- Skills specify their own output paths in active/ (e.g. active/model-chat/, active/leads/)
- Periodic cleanup: "clean up active/ folder, temp files delete, others organize into logical subfolders"
- VS Code color-coded folders: settings.json in each workspace sets different header bar color (green = personal, default = business) — instant visual context switch
- Duplicate claude.md → agents.md + gemini.md for model-agnostic portability
- CRM as JSON file in workspace, sync to GitHub nightly via cron job option

chunks.title: Security
chunks.rawContent:
Low-hanging fruit security fixes (80/20 approach):

1. API key leakage: ALL Claude Code conversations stored as JSONL files at ~/.claude/ on your computer. Any API key pasted in plain text chat = stored in these logs forever. Fix: always use .env file, reference via environment variable, never paste keys directly in chat.
Demo: Nick showed searching ~/.claude/ for a specific word and finding the exact conversation transcript.

2. npm package hallucination attacks: AI models hallucinate package names (acorn → acorns). Attackers register misspelled packages containing malware that exfiltrates your ~/.claude/ conversation logs and API keys. Fix: always audit npm dependencies before install, ask Claude "are there any unfamiliar packages here that aren't legitimate verified packages?"

3. Supabase RLS (Row Level Security): disabled by default. Without it, any signed-in user can read/write/delete EVERY row in your database. MoltBook example: no RLS = security researcher read all AI agent profiles + created 100,000 fake ones in seconds. Meta bought them anyway. Fix: enable RLS immediately on every Supabase project, takes 2 seconds.

4. Public VPS exposure: any public-facing server on a hosting provider IP range gets constant bot scanning (tens of thousands of requests/second from bot farms). Never put sensitive data on a raw public server without firewall + authentication.

5. Never store credit card data: Stripe handles all PCI compliance. If Claude reads a credit card number it goes into conversation logs = exfiltration risk.

Security audit workflow: run security audit prompt against codebase using a FRESH agent (not the dev agent — avoid bias). Then implement fixes using a DIFFERENT model (CodeX/Gemini) to avoid implementation bias. Nick provides free security audit prompt in his vibe coding course.

chunks.title: The Future of Claude & Agentic Engineering
chunks.rawContent:
Intelligence curve: going vertical. Chimpanzee → human → Einstein gap is surprisingly small in neuron count. We're crossing from chimpanzee-level to human-level AI rapidly. Once models can use human-level intelligence to improve their own training, curve goes truly vertical.

Decreasing human involvement trend:
- Vibe coding (2024-2025): human prompts → AI writes → human reviews
- Agentic engineering (now): human directs agent teams
- Auto-research: human sets goal, agents run autonomously
- Full independent research: agents set their own direction (coming)
- End state: CEO of agent fleet model — humans as principal investigators, agents as research assistants

Auto mode in Claude Code: new permission mode where Claude manages its own permissions automatically instead of asking. Small example of the broader trend toward less human approval needed.

Software moat shift: used to be quality of software. Now: "can build Netflix in 5 minutes with 3-4 agents on fast mode." New moats = distribution, compliance certifications, legal rights/licenses, hard-won customer relationships. SaaS subscription model declining — why pay $199/month when you can spend $19 + 30 minutes of tokens to build it yourself?

Economic implications: leverage going up dramatically. 1 person doing 100 tasks instead of 100 people doing 1 task each. Eventually may require new economic system. William Gibson: "The future is here, it's just unevenly distributed."

Who wins: sub-1% of population understands agent harnesses. You are in that group. Asymmetric productivity advantage. Even 0.1% ownership of the AI-enabled future = massive personal abundance given the unbounded upside.

chunks.title: Business Applications & Making Money
chunks.rawContent:
Nick's business context: $4M+/year profit using Claude Code daily. Teaches 2,000 people. Previous: 1SecondCopy content company hit 7 figures using Make.com automation.

Maker School (skool.com/makerschool): $184/mo, 218 videos, 2,100+ members, 90-day guarantee to land first client. Focuses on AI automation agency model — building systems for clients using Make.com, n8n, Claude.

Business use cases demonstrated in this course:
- Website optimization (auto-research on Lighthouse scores)
- Cold email automation (HTTP requests + browser automation, 6-10 tests/day at 500-1000 emails)
- Lead generation (browser automation to scrape + contact prospects)
- Content repurposing (daily news summaries, email reading, social media)
- Client workspace management (separate folders per client with their own .env, skills, claude.md)
- Document processing (reading emails, generating reports)
- Web scraping at scale (HTTP requests to 400+ websites simultaneously)

Pricing intelligence from video:
- Opus 4.6: $5/M input tokens (use for synthesis, QA, orchestration)
- Sonnet 4.6: $3/M input tokens (use for research, bulk tasks = 60% cheaper)
- Haiku: cheapest (use for simple data extraction, high volume)
- Browser Use platform: ~$100 flat + credits
- Conductor: separate pricing for parallel agent management

Agency model advice: start with browser automation to prototype → convert to HTTP for scale → sell the system to client for $2-5K/mo retainer. Workspace organization designed specifically for multi-client management.
